title: Load Balancing
summary: L4 vs L7 load balancing, algorithms, consistent hashing, health checks, and sticky sessions.

sections.content:
## What is Load Balancing?

A load balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple backend servers to maximize throughput, minimize latency, and avoid overloading any single server.

### Two layers:
- **L4 (Transport Layer)** — routes based on IP address and TCP/UDP port. Fast but dumb — no content awareness.
- **L7 (Application Layer)** — routes based on HTTP content: URL path, headers, cookies. Slower but powerful.

sections.title: L4 vs L7 Load Balancing
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph L4 Load Balancer
        C1[Client] -->|TCP connection| L4[L4 LB\nIP:Port routing]
        L4 --> S1[Server 1]
        L4 --> S2[Server 2]
        Note1[Sees: IP, Port\nNot: URL, Headers]
    end
    subgraph L7 Load Balancer
        C2[Client] -->|HTTP request| L7[L7 LB\nContent-based routing]
        L7 -->|/api/*| API[API Servers]
        L7 -->|/static/*| CDN[CDN / Static]
        L7 -->|/ws/*| WS[WebSocket Servers]
    end
    style L7 fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style L4 fill:#d97706,color:#fff

## Load Balancing Algorithms

| Algorithm | How it works | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| **Round Robin** | Each server in turn | Equal capacity servers |
| **Weighted Round Robin** | More traffic to more powerful servers | Mixed capacity |
| **Least Connections** | Routes to server with fewest active connections | Variable request duration |
| **IP Hash** | Hash client IP → same server | Sticky sessions |
| **Random** | Random server selection | Simple, low overhead |
| **Resource Based** | Routes based on server CPU/memory | Adaptive workloads |

sections.title: Consistent Hashing Ring
sections.content:
graph TD
    Ring(["Hash Ring\n0 ... 2^32"])
    Ring --> N1[Node A\nat position 100]
    Ring --> N2[Node B\nat position 200]
    Ring --> N3[Node C\nat position 300]
    Key1["Key 'user:1'\nhash=150"] -->|goes to next node clockwise| N2
    Key2["Key 'user:2'\nhash=250"] -->|goes to next node clockwise| N3
    Key3["Key 'user:3'\nhash=350"] -->|wraps around| N1
    style Ring fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Consistent Hashing

Regular hashing: `server = hash(key) % N` — when N changes (add/remove server), almost ALL keys remap.

Consistent hashing: place servers and keys on a virtual ring. Each key maps to the next server clockwise. Adding/removing a server only remaps keys from its **immediate neighbor** — minimizing cache invalidation.

**Virtual nodes** (vnodes): each physical server occupies multiple positions on the ring for better distribution.

```
Without consistent hashing: add 1 server → 75% of cache keys remap
With consistent hashing:    add 1 server → ~1/N of cache keys remap
```

Used by: Cassandra, DynamoDB, Redis Cluster, CDN routing.

## Health Checks

Load balancers continuously check server health:

- **Passive health check** — observe actual traffic; mark down after N failures
- **Active health check** — ping `/health` endpoint every 5s; mark down on timeout

```nginx
upstream backend {
    server app1:8080;
    server app2:8080;
    server app3:8080;
    
    keepalive 32;  # persistent connections
}

# Active health check (nginx plus)
health_check interval=5s fails=3 passes=2 uri=/actuator/health;
```

## Sticky Sessions

Some applications need requests from the same user to always reach the same server (e.g. legacy session apps). Use **cookie-based affinity**:

```
LB sets cookie: SERVERID=app1
Subsequent requests include cookie → LB routes to app1
```

⚠️ Sticky sessions defeat load balancing when one user has heavy traffic. Prefer stateless design.

sections.question: What problem does consistent hashing solve compared to regular modulo hashing?
options: It distributes load more evenly across servers
It minimizes the number of keys that need to be remapped when servers are added or removed
It prevents hot spots in the hash ring
It allows servers to communicate without a central coordinator
sections.explanation: With regular modulo hashing (key % N), changing N (adding/removing a server) causes almost all keys to remap to different servers — catastrophic for caches. Consistent hashing places both keys and servers on a ring so only ~1/N of keys need remapping when a server is added or removed.
